name: Cueva de la Virgen del Agua y el Pilón Azul - circular by the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
name_original: Cueva de la Virgen del Agua y el Pilón Azul - circular por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
region_intro: Hiking around Quesada offers diverse landscapes across two distinct regions in Spain. The municipality of Quesada in Jaén, Andalusia, is situated within the vast Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, characterized by rugged karst mountains, deep valleys, and significant river systems like the Guadalquivir. Further east, Ciudad Quesada in Alicante provides trails with views of unique salt lakes, agricultural areas along the Segura River, and hills featuring historical cave dwellings. The terrain varies from challenging mountain paths to more relaxed walks through natural parks. Best hiking trails around Quesada The most popular hiking route is Río Cerezuelo Trail, a 3.0 miles (4.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 45 minutes to complete. This difficult route leads through lush riverine landscapes, past historical ruins, and towards the Malena Waterfall. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cueva de la Virgen del Agua y el Pilón Azul - circular by the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, a moderate 5.1 miles (8.3 km) path. This circular trail explores features within the Natural Park, including the Cave of el Agua. Local hikers also love the Salinas del Mesto Trail, a 7.3 miles (11.7 km) trail leading through varied terrain, offering views from Cerro de Atayuela, often completed in about 3 hours 17 minutes. Hiking around Quesada is defined by karst mountains, riverine landscapes, and unique salt lakes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging ascents to more relaxed walks. The routes in Quesada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 300 reviews. More than 2,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Quesada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Quesada?
A: There are over 80 hiking trails around Quesada, catering to various skill levels. You'll find approximately 28 easy routes, 39 moderate, and 14 difficult trails, ensuring options for every hiker.

Q: What types of landscapes can I expect when hiking in Quesada?
A: Quesada offers two distinct hiking experiences. In &lt;strong&gt;Quesada, Jaén&lt;/strong&gt;, within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, expect rugged karst mountains, deep valleys, and significant river systems like the Guadalquivir. In &lt;strong&gt;Ciudad Quesada, Alicante&lt;/strong&gt;, trails offer views of unique salt lakes (like the Pink Lake of Torrevieja), agricultural areas along the Segura River, and hills featuring historical cave dwellings.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the Quesada area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984902363/cueva-de-la-virgen-del-agua-y-el-pilon-azul-circular-by-the-p-n-de-las-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de la Virgen del Agua y el Pilón Azul - circular by the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 5.1-mile path that explores features within the Natural Park, including the Cave of el Agua.

Q: What are some natural attractions or landmarks I can see while hiking?
A: The region boasts several impressive natural attractions. In Quesada, Jaén, you can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3983035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Guadalquivir River&lt;/a&gt;, the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/183641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Gilillo&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic views, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malena Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. Near Ciudad Quesada, Alicante, you can enjoy views of the Salt Lakes of Torrevieja and La Mata, known for flamingos, or explore the historical 'Cuevas de Rodeo' in nearby Rojales.

Q: Are there any hikes that feature waterfalls?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979725386/rio-cerezuelo-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Cerezuelo Trail&lt;/a&gt; in Quesada, Jaén, is a difficult 3.0-mile hike that leads through lush riverine landscapes and culminates at the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malena Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1062504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linarejos Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into various routes in the Sierras de Cazorla Natural Park.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Quesada?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 300 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse terrain, from challenging mountain paths to relaxed walks, and the stunning natural beauty, particularly the karst mountains, riverine landscapes, and unique salt lakes.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Quesada?
A: Yes, both regions offer suitable options. In Ciudad Quesada, Alicante, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Recorral Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; provides small paths ideal for scenic family walks. In Quesada, Jaén, while some trails are more challenging, there are easier sections within the natural park that are suitable for families looking for less strenuous outings.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails?
A: Many trails in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park (Quesada, Jaén) and around Ciudad Quesada, Alicante, are dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected natural areas, to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Check specific trail regulations if you plan to visit a very sensitive area.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly hikes?
A: Yes, Quesada offers a good selection of easier routes. Approximately 28 of the trails are rated as easy. For instance, in Ciudad Quesada, the paths within El Recorral Natural Park or along the Segura River are generally flat and suitable for beginners or those seeking a relaxed stroll.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Quesada?
A: The best time for hiking in Quesada, Jaén, is typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder and the landscapes are lush. Summers can be very hot, especially in the interior. For Ciudad Quesada, Alicante, the coastal climate makes hiking pleasant for a longer period, from early spring to late autumn, with winters also being mild enough for walks.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. In the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park (Quesada, Jaén), designated parking areas are often available near popular trail starting points. For trails around Ciudad Quesada, Alicante, you can often find parking in residential areas or near natural parks like El Recorral. It's advisable to check specific route information for details on parking.

Q: Are there any long-distance hiking routes that pass through Quesada?
A: Yes, the impressive GR 7, a long-distance hiking route that crosses Spain, has a section traversing the area around Quesada, Jaén. This offers opportunities for multi-day treks or longer day hikes along a well-established path.
tips: Canyon with vertical walls with numerous waterfalls through which the Tíscar River flows with all the power and beauty of the water. It is prepared with walkways and wooden railings for safety and good care of the area.
The Blue Pilon is between the villages of Don Pedro and Belerda. The waterfall is formed by the Tíscar river, which comes down from the incredible Cueva del Agua and creates this waterfall.

waypoints.name: Cave of the Virgin of Tíscar and Waterfall
